museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Nintendo launches the Famicom, Sega launches the SG-1000, Mario Bros. reaches Japan, Manic Miner arrives for the Spectrum, and the future quietly changes shape on both sides of the world.
museum.curatorNote: July is unusually well anchored. The Japanese console dates are solid; some UK software dates are year or month-level depending on source, so those entries stay modest.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: July gives 1983 a new centre of gravity.

items.title: Nintendo launches the Family Computer in Japan
items.body: The Famicom arrived with Donkey Kong, Donkey Kong Jr. and Popeye, years before the machine's redesigned western life as the NES.
items.sourceNote: Nintendo/Famicom histories list July 15, 1983 and the three launch games.

items.title: Sega launches the SG-1000 in Japan
items.body: Sega's first home console launched the same day as the Famicom, making July 15 a quietly enormous date for Japanese hardware history.

items.title: Mario Bros. releases in Japan
items.body: Nintendo's pipe-platform cabinet gave Mario and Luigi a clearer shared identity just as Nintendo was preparing its home-console future.
items.sourceNote: Mario Bros. references list July 14, 1983 in Japan.

items.title: Manic Miner gives the Spectrum a platforming myth
items.body: Matthew Smith's game became a defining UK microcomputer object: strange rooms, bright screens, unforgiving jumps and bedroom-programmer legend.
items.sourceNote: Manic Miner references list 1983 and many summaries place release in July.

items.title: The crash looks less universal from Japan and Britain
items.body: North American consoles were collapsing, but Japan launched new consoles and Britain leaned into micros. The global story was splitting.
items.sourceNote: Synthesis from Famicom, SG-1000 and UK micro context.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight July arrivals and launch-window objects.

items.title: Family Computer
items.body: Nintendo's Famicom begins its Japanese life as a cartridge console attached to the family television.

items.title: Donkey Kong
items.body: One of the three Famicom launch games, translating Nintendo's arcade credibility into the home.

items.title: Donkey Kong Jr.
items.body: A second arcade conversion in the Famicom's launch set.

items.title: Popeye
items.body: The licensed cartoon cabinet becomes a launch cartridge.

items.title: SG-1000
items.body: Sega's first console enters the same market on the same date as Nintendo's Famicom.

items.title: Mario Bros.
items.body: Mario and Luigi fight pests from below in a single-screen arena.

items.title: Manic Miner
items.body: A landmark British platformer with surreal screens and exacting jumps.
items.sourceNote: Manic Miner references vary in precision; July is widely cited.

items.title: Elevator Action
items.body: Taito's lift-and-door spy game reaches a wider arcade audience after Japanese release.
items.certainty: regional month supported by secondary sources
items.sourceNote: Elevator Action references list June Japan and July North America.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: July's display case is a console counter-history.

items.title: Nintendo Famicom
items.body: Cheap-looking, powerful enough, and software-led. Its 1983 Japanese launch would matter far beyond the month itself.

items.title: Sega SG-1000
items.body: Sega's first home console, launched on the same day as the Famicom and tied to Sega's broader home-hardware path.

items.title: ZX Spectrum 48K
items.body: Manic Miner made the Spectrum feel like a native games machine rather than a cheap computer playing dress-up.

items.title: Arcade cabinet as Nintendo pipeline
items.body: Mario Bros. shows the arcade-to-console pipeline forming before western players recognised the pattern.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: July's paper record would have felt ahead and behind at once.

items.title: Electronic Games
items.headline: The console crash, seen from America
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for July 1983.
items.curatorNote: A US magazine could not yet know how important Nintendo's Japanese launch would become.
items.body: A US magazine could not yet know how important Nintendo's Japanese launch would become.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: Manic Miner and the summer of Spectrum play
items.curatorNote: For UK readers, Manic Miner was nearer and more tangible than the Famicom.
items.body: For UK readers, Manic Miner was nearer and more tangible than the Famicom.

items.title: Your Computer
items.headline: The home computer as games platform
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Your Computer cover for July 1983.
items.curatorNote: The UK computer press made platform choice feel like cultural identity.
items.body: The UK computer press made platform choice feel like cultural identity.

items.title: ZX Computing
items.headline: Spectrum rooms, jumps and cassette myth
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed ZX Computing cover for July 1983.
items.curatorNote: The Spectrum magazine shelf was ready for Miner Willy.
items.body: The Spectrum magazine shelf was ready for Miner Willy.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: The most important news did not arrive evenly.

items.title: Japan's future was distant
items.body: Most UK players would not experience the Famicom launch directly.

items.title: Tapes travelled faster than consoles
items.body: A Spectrum game could become schoolyard knowledge quickly.

items.title: Magazine lag shaped reality
items.body: A July event might become a reader's fact weeks or months later.

items.title: Local play defined importance
items.body: Manic Miner mattered because it was on machines people nearby owned.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: July felt like several futures starting separately.

items.title: Japan rebooted the console idea
items.body: The Famicom and SG-1000 launched while the US console market struggled.

items.title: Britain had its own hero
items.body: Manic Miner made the Spectrum feel proudly local.

items.title: Mario moved closer to centre stage
items.body: Mario Bros. made him more than the man from Donkey Kong.

items.title: The crash became regional
items.body: One country's disaster was not the whole medium's fate.
